abstract: This paper extends Guess et al.'s (2021) US-based study of news quality on Facebook by analyzing the prevalence, exposure, and sharing of trustworthy and untrustworthy URLs in Germany, France, and Italy between 2019 and 2022. Using Meta's URL Shares Dataset combined with NewsGuard domain trustworthiness ratings, the authors document a decline in total URLs shared, a rising percentage of untrustworthy URLs (especially in electoral years in Germany and Italy), but relatively stable proportions of Views from untrustworthy sources. The study also confirms that older age cohorts dominate both exposure to and sharing of untrustworthy content, while younger users (<34) play a minimal role.
key_claims: The total number of URLs in Facebook's URL Shares Dataset has declined steadily from 2019 to 2022 across France, Germany, and Italy.

The percentage of URLs from untrustworthy sources increased in Germany (peaking at 21% in 2021) and Italy (peaking at 22% in 2022), corresponding with national election years, while declining in France.
Increases in the share of untrustworthy URLs and Shares do not translate into proportional increases in Views from untrustworthy sources, which remain relatively stable.
Older Facebook users (35-64, and especially 45+) are disproportionately responsible for exposure to and sharing of untrustworthy content, while users under 34 play a marginal role.

Methodological characteristics of NewsGuard data and the URL Shares Dataset (especially the 100-share threshold and US-skewed source coverage) make direct US-EU comparisons unreliable, justifying a focus on European data.
methods: Replication and extension of Guess et al. (2021), using publicly available code (Aslett, 2022) adapted to European contexts.
Analysis of Meta's URL Shares Dataset v10.1 (covering Jan 2017 - Oct 2022), filtered by 'top_country' for Germany, France, Italy, and the US.
Use of NewsGuard ratings (October 2022 snapshot of 8,506 rated domains) to classify domains as Trustworthy (score >=60) or Untrustworthy (<60), applied retrospectively.
Comparison of NewsGuard coverage with alternative datasets (Lasser-Rupp list, Media Bias/Fact Check, Grinberg et al. 2019) to assess representativeness.
Analysis of NewsGuard historical data to check rating stability, finding only ~1-3% of domains changed category over 2019-2022.
Calculation of confidence intervals around Views and Shares to account for differential privacy noise (σ=14 for shares, σ=2,228 for views).
Disaggregation of Views and Shares by six age cohorts (18-24, 25-34, 35-44, 45-54, 55-64, 65+).
findings: France consistently has the highest URL volume on Facebook, followed by Italy; Germany has roughly half or less of France's volume, consistent with lower Facebook news adoption in Germany (17% vs 45% in Italy per Reuters 2022).
In 2019, untrustworthy URL shares were similar (~13-14%) across the three countries; by 2022 they diverged sharply (France 11%, Germany ~21%, Italy 22%).
Absolute Views and Shares for untrustworthy URLs peaked in 2020 (notably in France and Italy) before declining, mirroring the overall URL decline.
The percentage of Views from untrustworthy sources stayed stable or slightly declined despite the rising percentage of untrustworthy URLs, suggesting platform-level dampening of exposure.
The percentage of Shares to untrustworthy domains rose substantially in Germany (22% in 2021) and Italy (26% in 2022).
Sharing of untrustworthy content skews older than exposure: 65+ dominates Sharing in France (28%), 55-64 in Germany (36%), and 45-54 in Italy (33%).

US data show a stronger positive correlation between the share of untrustworthy domains in NewsGuard and untrustworthy URLs in the dataset than European data, motivating the EU-only comparative focus.
contributions: Provides the first systematic quantitative description of trustworthy vs. untrustworthy news circulation on Facebook in three major European countries using Meta's privacy-protected URL Shares Dataset.
Extends Guess et al. (2021) temporally (through 2022) and geographically (to the EU), enabling cross-national comparison within Europe.
Offers a methodological critique of the URL Shares Dataset (notably the 100-share threshold) and of using NewsGuard data longitudinally, including stability checks of ratings over time.
Documents age-based patterns of misinformation engagement in European contexts, extending US findings about older users' outsized role in sharing untrustworthy content.
Highlights implications for upcoming EU Digital Services Act transparency obligations regarding platform research access.
framing: The paper situates itself within the post-2016 research agenda on misinformation, platform exposure measurement, and the methodological challenges of studying news consumption on social media. It engages with industry-academic data partnership initiatives (Social Science One, Meta's URL Shares Dataset) and positions itself as a comparative, European counterpoint to US-centric quantitative studies of platform-mediated information quality.
